steps.text: Configure the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management by connecting a heated transfer probe, a heated filter, an impinger conditioning system, an unheated transfer line and the unheated critical orifice and connecting fittings to a bypass pump.
Soak glassware and sampling apparatus in hot soapy water (Alconox® or equivalent) at 50 °C or higher.
Rinse glassware three times with hot tap water.
Rinse glassware three times with DI water.
Rinse glassware three times with methanol.
Determine which sampling system configuration is appropriate for sampling.
Determine stack gas moisture content.
For non-combustion sources, determine if acid gases are suspected or are present based on process knowledge.
Use the VFC Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management.
Use the Direct VFC Sampling System.
Use the VFC Canister Sampling System with Multiple Canisters.
Prepare the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid-Gas Management impinger conditioning system.
Prepare the Direct VFC Sampling System.

preconditions.text: If the source is a combustion source, the moisture content is assumed to be >3% v/v and water/acid gas management is required.
If the moisture is confirmed to be ≤3% v/v and acid gases are not suspected, use the Direct VFC Sampling System.
If emissions stream may contain unknown concentrations or VFC concentrations above the analysis calibration range, use the VFC Canister Sampling System with Multiple Canisters.

steps.text: Prepare the Direct VFC Sampling System.
Connect the sample probe liner to the heated glass filter housing with a QFF or GFF.
Connect the exit of the heated glass filter housing to the impinger conditioning system if water/acid gas management is needed.
Connect the appropriate transfer line in series with the critical orifice tee and the tubing to the bypass pump.
Heat the sampling train once the sample extraction system assembly is complete.

preconditions.text: If the conditions listed in Section 8.1 for direct sampling are met, the canister sample can be collected directly from the source without the impinger water/acid gas management system.

steps.text: Form a post-test leak check.
Disconnect the canister from the train.
Confirm the final canister vacuum.
Attach the vacuum gauge to the canister and open the canister valve.
Record the final pressure.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume to the theoretical volume.
Close the valve, remove the gauge, and replace the canister inlet cap.
Collect the required data for each sample.

preconditions.text: A RPD > 20% indicates a potential leak or a clogged orifice and requires resampling.

steps.text: Determine the sampling system background.
Setup, heat, and leak check the sampling system as described in Sections 8.5 and 8.6.
Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.

Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate. Purge the system for at least two minutes.
Collect the sample as described in Section 8.6. Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister.
Submit the canister for analysis as a sample.

Check the field integrity of collected samples. Perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 8.6.2.8. Flag data that differ between the two approaches by ≥20% RPD and indicate if leakage is suspected and invalidate the sample with a similar explanation.

Check the laboratory integrity of collected field samples. Upon receipt in the laboratory perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 11.1.2. Flag data that differs between the two approaches by more than 5% RPD and indicate leakage is suspected or invalidate the data with a similar explanation.
Provide COC information. Refer to Section 8.6.5 for the minimum information to provide.
Gas dilution systems used to generate calibration gases shall be capable of producing standards whose measured values are within ±3 percent of predicted values.

Annual evaluations are required to verify that the gas dilution system is contamination-free and not biasing samples. Perform gas dilution system evaluations by generating and analyzing blank samples using humidified zero air.
Perform mass flow or pressure gauge audits on a yearly basis to verify the accuracy of the system used to dilute calibration standards and samples.
Perform mass flow or pressure gauge audits on a yearly basis to verify the accuracy of the system used to dilute calibration standards and samples.
Recalibrate dynamic gas dilution systems once per year using NIST traceable primary flow standards with an uncertainty of ≤ 0.25 %.
Recalibrate static gas dilution systems once per year using a NIST traceable primary pressure gauge with an uncertainty of ≤ 0.1 %.
Ensure static gas dilution systems have pressure transducer(s) with an accuracy of ± 0.1 % full scale or 0.13 kPa, whichever is smaller, calibrated over the range of use for the application.

Maintain records of calibration results, listing the date of the most recent calibration, the reference flow or calibration device identification and serial number as well as the measured performance that demonstrates meeting the uncertainty criteria.
Leak check GC/MS systems by monitoring the mass spectrometer vacuum or performing an air/water background check prior to sample analysis.
Demonstrate and document ability to perform the method by performing the IDC actions.

preconditions.text: Prior to conducting the IDC, the analyst must meet the calibration requirements outlined in Section 10.

steps.text: Prepare a humidified CCV standard in a canister at a concentration in the lower third of the calibration curve.
Analyze a CCV standard to verify the ICAL for each target compound following each successful ICAL.
Analyze a daily CCV at the beginning of each analytical sequence.
Using the daily CCV to calculate sample concentrations.

preconditions.text: If secondary certified calibration standards are unavailable, individual targeted VFCs can be purchased and prepared at nominal concentrations from the pure/neat compounds.
Failure to meet the ±20% agreement requires corrective action such as instrument maintenance, repreparation of the CCV, and recalibration.

steps.text: Analyze a CCV at the end of the analytical sequence and after every ten samples, whichever is more frequent.
Perform corrective action and reanalyze all samples not bracketed by a passing CCV for the failing compound(s).
Each analytical sequence must include analysis of one replicate field sample.
Analyze one canister from every cleaning batch as an LB to ensure that the cleaning procedure is adequately removing contaminants.
Evacuate clean passivated stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent, to 50 mTorr then inject 50 µL of DI water using a 100-microliter glass displacement syringe.

preconditions.text: If a sequence continuing or ending CCV fails to meet the criterion, perform corrective action and reanalyze all samples not bracketed by a passing CCV for the failing compound(s).
Target compounds with concentrations >3x the MDL must agree within ±25% RPD in the replicate field samples.

steps.text: Evacuate clean passivated stainless-steel canisters, or equivalent, to 50 mTorr then inject 50 µL of DI water using a 100-microliter glass displacement syringe.
Prepare VFC calibration standards by diluting individual neat standards and/or from vendor certified pressurized cylinders containing a mixture of the VFC standard targets in nitrogen.
Determine the calibration concentration range needed to span the measured sample concentrations.
Use individual canister calibration standards and the same injection volume to generate calibration curves.
Use either a dynamic or static gas dilution system, or equivalent technique, to blend the VFC calibration gas with zero air.
After preparation, allow the pressurized calibration standard canisters to equilibrate at ambient laboratory temperature for a minimum of 12 hours prior to instrumental analysis.

Calibration gases may be generated by dynamic dilution of a certified compressed gas cylinder containing VFC targets in nitrogen to make a working standard that is diluted with a gas stream of humidified zero air in the heated mixing chamber to generate various concentrations of calibration standards.
Use the stack equivalent concentration of CO2 determined using an ancillary measurement to determine the percentage of CO2 to add to these calibration standards.
Analyze the two standards containing CO2 using the same injection volume that will be used to analyze samples.
Prepare CO2 standards in a way to yield the expected concentration at the injection volume used for sample canisters.
Ensure recovery of each target compound in the two standards containing CO2 is within ±30% of the true value.
Flag a quantitative value for any VFC that exceeds ±30% with an explanation about CO2 interference.
Receive canister samples from the field and cross check COC forms with canister identification numbers.
Resolve any disparities before analyzing canisters.
Analyze seven replicate injections of a canister spiked at a midpoint of the calibration curve.
Check for leaks in the analytical system, repair and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the standard preparation system.
Analyze seven replicate injections of a canister spiked at a midpoint of the calibration curve.
Check for leaks in the analytical system, repair and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the standard preparation system.
Check for instrument leaks and reanalyze.
Prepare fresh standard and reanalyze.
Check for leaks in the chromatographic system, repair and reanalyze.
Flag any sample data that does not meet IS retention time criteria.
Check for leaks in the analytical system, perform maintenance and reanalyze.
Retune and recalibrate instrument and reanalyze.
Flag any sample results with failing IS responses.
